# HDVS â High Density Voltage Supply

The **HDVS (High Density Voltage Supply)** is an instrument board for the **Advantest V93000 ATE platform** (originally Teradyne). It is a high-density, high-channel-count power supply designed for testing devices with current consumption up to **4A**.

---

## Key Specifications

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Channels** | 48 nonfloating voltage supplies per board |
| **Output Voltage Range** | 0â7V |
| **Current per Channel (Single)** | Up to 1A |
| **Dual Merge Mode** | Up to 2A |
| **Quad Merge Mode** | Up to 4A |
| **Parameter Sets (PSets)** | 64 |
| **Capture Memory** | 2K per channel |
| **Strobe Memory** | 1K per channel |
| **Capture Sampling Frequency** | Min: 1,356 Hz â Max: 5,555,555 Hz |
| **Decoupling** | Internal switchable 1F decoupling capacitor |
| **Connection** | Per-channel Kelvin connect |

[Source: HDVS/HDVS_HDVSDefTopic.pdf, Page 1â2; HDVS/HDVS_hdvsabout.1.2.pdf, Page 2]

---

## Features

- **Flexible merging configurations**: Single (1A), Dual (2A), Quad (4A) modes allow scaling current delivery to the DUT.
- **Pattern-controlled measurement**: Supports fast **IDDQ static current measurement** testing with pattern-controlled voltage supply parameter settings for main and alternate voltage levels.
- **Go/No-Go test capability**.
- **Simultaneous capture and strobe**: The 2K capture memory and 1K strobe memory are separate and can be used simultaneously (e.g., strobing supply current while capturing supply voltage).
- **On-board calibration resources**.
- **Digital capture sampling rate and sample size** are programmable.

[Source: HDVS/HDVS_hdvsabout.1.2.pdf, Page 2]

---

## Hardware Architecture

The HDVS board assembly resides in the **test head** and consists of:

- **12 module boards** â each containing the analog circuitry for **4 DCVS channels** (4 Ă 12 = 48 total channels). Channels on a module board can be **merged** to increase output current/power.
- **1 main board** â contains the digital control logic, bus interface, and the DCVS output stage (attached to the cooling assembly). **Six Slice FPGAs** on the main board each support two module boards and implement the **Digital Control Loop (DCL)**.
- **1 power board** â provides DC-DC power conversion.

From a **programming perspective**, the HDVS board is viewed as a set of one or more independent **DCVS instruments**, programmed using **VBT syntax** (DCVS API).

[Source: HDVS/HDVS_hdvstheory.2.2.pdf, Page 1; HDVS/HDVS_hdvstheory.2.1.pdf, Page 1]

---

## Documentation Sections Available

The HDVS documentation covers:
- Theory of Operation (board hardware, test head connections, functional description, output power)
- DCVS Programming
- DCVS Debug Display
- HDVS Examples
- HDVS DIB Design Guidelines
- HDVS Specifications
- Safety Information & Calibration

[Source: HDVS/HDVS_HDVSDefTopic.pdf, Page 1â2]

---

## Practical Notes / ADI Best Practices

- **IDDQ testing**: HDVS is well-suited for static current measurement (IDDQ) on ADI devices â leverage the pattern-controlled storage for fast multi-site IDDQ screening.
- **Merging**: When your DUT requires >1A on a supply rail (common for ADI power management or mixed-signal devices), use dual or quad merge mode. Ensure your **HIB routing** supports the merged channel topology.
- **Kelvin connections**: Always use the per-channel Kelvin sense lines for accurate voltage regulation at the DUT, especially for low-voltage/high-current supplies.
- **Capture memory**: The 2K capture depth at up to ~5.56 MHz sampling is useful for transient supply characterization but is limited â for deeper waveform capture, consider complementary instruments.
- **Sampling frequency for strobe**: Note that the programmed sampling frequency is **ignored for strobe** â strobe timing is pattern-controlled.

If you need details on specific HDVS programming (DCVS API calls), DIB design guidelines, or merging configuration examples, let me know!

# HDVS â High Density Voltage Supply

The **HDVS (High Density Voltage Supply)** is a Teradyne UltraFlex instrument card designed as a high-density, high-channel-count DC power supply for device testing. Here is a comprehensive overview:

---

## Overview

The HDVS provides **48 non-floating voltage supply channels** per board, with an output voltage range of **0â7V at up to 1A per channel (unmerged)**. It is located in the UltraFlex test head and is commonly used for powering DUT supply pins during production test and characterization.

[Source: HDVS/HDVS_HDVSDefTopic.pdf, Page 1]

---

## Key Features

| Feature | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Channels per board** | 48 DCVS power supplies |
| **Voltage range** | 0V â 7V |
| **Current (unmerged)** | Up to 1A per channel |
| **Merging modes** | Single (1A), Dual (2A), Quad (4A) |
| **PSets** | 64 per channel |
| **Kelvin connect** | Per channel |
| **Internal decoupling** | Switchable 1F decoupling capacitor |
| **Capture memory** | 2K per channel |
| **Strobe memory** | 1K per channel (separate from capture, can be used simultaneously) |
| **Capture sampling frequency** | Min: 1,356 Hz, Max: 5,555,555 Hz |
| **IDDQ support** | Pattern-controlled measurement storage for fast IDDQ static current testing |
| **Go/No-Go testing** | Supported |
| **On-board calibration** | Yes |
| **Pattern-controlled** | Voltage supply parameter settings for main and alternate voltage levels |

[Source: HDVS/HDVS_hdvsabout.1.2.pdf, Page 2; HDVS/HDVS_HDVSDefTopic.pdf, Page 1]

---

## Hardware Architecture

The HDVS board assembly consists of:

- **12 module boards** â Each contains the analog circuitry for 4 DCVS channels (4 Ă 12 = 48 total channels). Channels on a module board can be **merged** to increase output current/power.
- **1 main board** â Contains the digital control logic, bus interface, and the DCVS output stage (attached to the cooling assembly). **Six Slice FPGAs** on the main board each support 2 module boards and implement the **Digital Control Loop (DCL)**.
- **1 power board** â Provides DC-DC power conversion for the instrument.

[Source: HDVS/HDVS_hdvstheory.2.2.pdf, Page 1]

---

## Specification Categories

The HDVS specifications cover:

- **Force Voltage**
- **Fold Current Limit**
- **Measure Voltage**
- **Measure Current**
- **Dynamic Performance**
- **Other**

> â ïž Specifications are guaranteed unless noted as *typical* (sample tested, not guaranteed) or *nominal* (calculated, not guaranteed).

[Source: HardwareSpecs/HardwareSpecs_hwJagHDVS.12.01.pdf, Page 1]

---

## Programming

From a programming perspective, the HDVS board is viewed as a set of independent **DCVS** instruments and is programmed using **VBT (Visual Basic for Test)** syntax via the `DCVS` object in IG-XL.

[Source: HDVS/HDVS_HDVSDefTopic.pdf, Page 2]

---

## ADI Best Practices

- **Use Kelvin connections** for accurate voltage forcing/measurement at the DUT, especially for low-voltage supplies.
- **Leverage merging** when DUT supply pins require >1A (e.g., VDD core supplies on devices like ADuCM410 or ADIN series).
- **Enable the internal 1F decoupling capacitor** for supplies that need low-noise, stable output; disable it for fast transient response testing.
- **Use capture + strobe simultaneously** for efficient IDDQ testing â strobe current while capturing voltage to detect defective states.
- **Manage PSets carefully** â with 64 PSets available, plan your test flow to minimize DCVS level changes and improve throughput.

---

# UltraPin1600 Card Overview

The **UltraPin1600** is a high-speed digital instrument card for the **Teradyne UltraFlex ATE platform**, designed for functional digital testing of semiconductor devices. Here is a comprehensive summary:

---

## Key Features

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Channels** | 256 single-ended channels (or 128 differential I/O pairs) |
| **Clock Speed** | 800 MHz |
| **Vector Memory (VM)** | Up to 512M vectors (UltraPin1600) / up to 1024M vectors (UltraPin1600+), depending on timing mode |
| **Subroutine Memory (SRM)** | Up to 8K, depending on timing mode |
| **Time Domains** | 1 time domain per 128 channels (2 time domains per board) |

[Source: UltraPin1600_UltraPin1600_about.1.2.pdf, Page 1/3]

---

## Board Architecture

The UltraPin1600 assembly is divided into **two blocks of 128 channels each**. Each block connects to its own **pattern generator**. Within each block, the 128 channels are organized into **groups of 32 channels**, and each group has its own:

- **Timing Generator**
- **Vector Memory (VM)**
- **Protocol Aware (PA)** functionality

Each individual channel has its own **levels control** and **PPMU (Pin Parametric Measurement Unit)**. The board connects to the tester computer via a **high-speed serial link** and interfaces with other instruments using a **Move/Sync Link**.

[Source: UltraPin1600_UltraPin1600_theory.2.03.pdf, Page 1/1]

---

## Digital Channel Composition

Each digital channel consists of:

- **Pin Electronics (PE)** â driver, detector, and termination circuit
- **Timing/Format Generator** â generates event signals to control drive and compare levels
- **Fail Processing** â accepts signals from comparators for pass/fail determination
- **HRAM (History RAM)** â captures data from pattern cycles
- **Vector Memory** â stores drive/detect data with timing information

[Source: UltraPin1600_UltraPin1600_theory.2.02.pdf, Page 1/2]

---

## Supported Protocols & Capabilities

- **Protocol Aware (PA)** functionality supporting:
  - Low-speed control buses: **IÂČC, MDIO, JTAG**
  - Dedicated **DDR2, DDR3, LPDDR1, LPDDR2** protocols
- **Digital Signal Source and Capture (DSSC)**:
  - Up to **two 32-bit digital signal source engines** per group of 64 channels (single mode)
  - **One digital signal capture engine** per group of 8 channels (single mode)
- **Memory Test Option (MTO)**
- **SCAN** instrument support
- **PPMU** for DC parametric measurements on each channel

[Source: UltraPin1600_UltraPin1600_about.1.2.pdf, Page 1/3]

---

## Additional Specifications Topics

The full specification document covers:
- General Board Specifications
- Channel Modes
- Timing & Edges
- Formats
- Pattern Data, Flow Control, Branch Conditions
- Pattern Start/Stop Control
- Pattern Communication with Test Program
- Pattern Control Bits
- Multiple Time Domains
- History RAM (HRAM)

[Source: HardwareSpecs_hwJagHSD1600.20.01.pdf, Page 1/3]

---

## ADI Best Practices

- **For SPI/IÂČC testing** of ADI devices (e.g., ADuCM410, ADIN6310), the Protocol Aware feature on UltraPin1600 is highly recommended as it simplifies pattern development for control bus communication.
- **When designing HIB (Handler Interface Board)**, follow the **UltraPin1600 DIB Design Guidelines** to ensure proper signal integrity at 800 MHz clock rates.
- **For mixed-signal ADI devices**, the UltraPin1600 is typically paired with analog instruments (HDVS, DCVI, HexVS) â ensure proper Move/Sync Link coordination between instruments for synchronized test execution.
- **Vector memory planning**: If your test program requires deep pattern depths (e.g., flash programming of ADuCM410), consider the **UltraPin1600+** variant with up to 1024M VM.

# Technical Document: UltraPin1600 Instrument Card Overview

**Document ID:** chipagent_h5.docx Analysis
**Author:** ADI ChipAgent
**Classification:** INTERNAL USE ONLY
**Date of Source Document:** April 27, 2026

---

## Executive Summary

The attached document, **chipagent_h5.docx**, is an internally authored ADI ChipAgent reference document that provides a comprehensive technical overview of the **Teradyne UltraPin1600** digital instrument card used on the **UltraFlex ATE platform**. It consolidates key specifications, board architecture details, digital channel composition, supported protocols, and ADI-specific best practices for test engineers working on ADI device validation and characterization. The document draws from multiple official Teradyne TDC (Test Development Cookbook) source PDFs and is intended for internal use within ADI's test engineering organization.

---

## Overview

The document covers the **UltraPin1600**, which is a high-density, high-speed digital pin electronics instrument card for the Teradyne UltraFlex semiconductor test platform. It is one of the primary digital instruments used by ADI for testing devices such as the **ADuCM410**, **ADIN6310**, **ADIN7210**, and **AMC038X** families.

The document is structured into the following major sections:

1. Key Features / Specifications
2. Board Architecture
3. Digital Channel Composition
4. Supported Protocols & Capabilities
5. Additional Specification Topics
6. ADI Best Practices

---

## Key Features and Specifications

The document presents the core specifications of the UltraPin1600 in a tabular format:

| **Parameter** | **Specification** |
|---|---|
| **Channels** | 256 single-ended channels (or 128 differential I/O pairs) |
| **Clock Speed** | **800 MHz** |
| **Vector Memory (VM)** | Up to **512M vectors** (UltraPin1600) / up to **1024M vectors** (UltraPin1600+), depending on timing mode |
| **Subroutine Memory (SRM)** | Up to **8K**, depending on timing mode |
| **Time Domains** | 1 time domain per 128 channels (**2 time domains per board**) |

[Source: UltraPin1600_UltraPin1600_about.1.2.pdf, Page 1/3]

> **Note:** The **UltraPin1600+** variant doubles the vector memory depth to **1024M vectors**, which is critical for deep pattern applications such as flash programming of the **ADuCM410**.

---

## Board Architecture

### Block Organization

The UltraPin1600 assembly is divided into **two blocks of 128 channels each**. Each block connects to its own dedicated **pattern generator**. Within each 128-channel block, channels are further organized into **groups of 32 channels**, where each group has its own:

- **Timing Generator** â independent timing control per 32-channel group
- **Vector Memory (VM)** â dedicated pattern storage per group
- **Protocol Aware (PA) functionality** â enabling protocol-level communication

### Channel-Level Resources

Each individual channel has its own:

- **Levels control** â independent voltage level programming
- **PPMU** (*Pin Parametric Measurement Unit*) â for DC parametric measurements

### Connectivity

- The board connects to the **tester computer** via a **high-speed serial link**
- It interfaces with other instruments (e.g., HDVS, DCVI, HexVS) using a **Move/Sync Link**

[Source: UltraPin1600_UltraPin1600_theory.2.03.pdf, Page 1/1]

---

## Digital Channel Composition

Each digital channel on the UltraPin1600 consists of five major functional blocks:

1. **Pin Electronics (PE)** â includes the *driver*, *detector*, and *termination circuit* for signal generation and reception
2. **Timing/Format Generator** â generates event signals that control drive and compare timing edges
3. **Fail Processing** â accepts signals from comparators for **pass/fail determination**
4. **HRAM (History RAM)** â captures data from pattern cycles for failure analysis and debug
5. **Vector Memory** â stores drive/detect data along with timing information

[Source: UltraPin1600_UltraPin1600_theory.2.02.pdf, Page 1/2]

---

## Supported Protocols and Capabilities

### Protocol Aware (PA)

The UltraPin1600 supports **Protocol Aware** functionality for the following protocols:

- **Low-speed control buses:**
  - `IÂČC`
  - `MDIO`
  - `JTAG`
- **Dedicated memory protocols:**
  - `DDR2`
  - `DDR3`
  - `LPDDR1`
  - `LPDDR2`

### Digital Signal Source and Capture (DSSC)

- Up to **two 32-bit digital signal source engines** per group of 64 channels (*single mode*)
- **One digital signal capture engine** per group of 8 channels (*single mode*)

### Additional Capabilities

- **Memory Test Option (MTO)** â for embedded memory testing
- **SCAN instrument support** â for scan-based structural testing
- **PPMU** â DC parametric measurements available on **every channel**

[Source: UltraPin1600_UltraPin1600_about.1.2.pdf, Page 1/3]

> **Important:** The Protocol Aware feature is confirmed in the internal knowledge base as a key capability of the UltraPin1600, alongside MTO, SCAN, DigSrc, DigCap, and PPMU.
> [Source: UltraPin1600_UltraPin1600DefTopic.pdf, Page 3/3]

---

## Additional Specification Topics

The document references a comprehensive specification document (likely **HardwareSpecs_hwJagHSD1600.20.01.pdf**) that covers the following detailed topics:

- General Board Specifications
- Channel Modes
- Timing & Edges
- Formats
- Pattern Data, Flow Control, Branch Conditions
- Pattern Start/Stop Control
- Pattern Communication with Test Program
- Pattern Control Bits
- Multiple Time Domains
- History RAM (HRAM)

[Source: HardwareSpecs_hwJagHSD1600.20.01.pdf, Page 1/3]

---

## ADI Best Practices

The document concludes with **ADI-specific recommendations** for using the UltraPin1600 in ADI device test programs:

### Protocol Aware for SPI/IÂČC Testing

> **Recommendation:** For SPI/IÂČC testing of ADI devices (e.g., **ADuCM410**, **ADIN6310**), the **Protocol Aware** feature on UltraPin1600 is *highly recommended* as it simplifies pattern development for control bus communication.

### HIB Design Guidelines

> **Recommendation:** When designing the **HIB** (*Handler Interface Board*), follow the **UltraPin1600 DIB Design Guidelines** to ensure proper signal integrity at **800 MHz** clock rates.

### Mixed-Signal Device Testing

> **Recommendation:** For mixed-signal ADI devices, the UltraPin1600 is typically paired with analog instruments (**HDVS**, **DCVI**, **HexVS**). Ensure proper **Move/Sync Link** coordination between instruments for synchronized test execution.

### Vector Memory Planning

> **Recommendation:** If your test program requires deep pattern depths (e.g., **flash programming of ADuCM410**), consider the **UltraPin1600+** variant with up to **1024M** vector memory.

---

The attached document aggregates information from the following internal Teradyne TDC source documents:

---

## Summary

**Key takeaways from the attached document (chipagent_h5.docx):**

- The document is an **ADI internal reference** summarizing the **Teradyne UltraPin1600** digital instrument card for the UltraFlex ATE platform.
- The UltraPin1600 provides **256 single-ended channels** at **800 MHz** with up to **512M vectors** (or **1024M** on the UltraPin1600+ variant).
- The board is organized into **two 128-channel blocks**, each with its own pattern generator, further subdivided into **32-channel groups** with independent timing, VM, and Protocol Aware engines.
- Each channel includes full **Pin Electronics**, **Timing/Format Generator**, **Fail Processing**, **HRAM**, and **PPMU**.
- **Protocol Aware** supports `IÂČC`, `MDIO`, `JTAG`, `DDR2`, `DDR3`, `LPDDR1`, and `LPDDR2`.
- ADI best practices emphasize using **Protocol Aware for SPI/IÂČC testing**, following **DIB Design Guidelines** for signal integrity, coordinating **Move/Sync Links** for mixed-signal testing, and selecting the **UltraPin1600+** for deep vector memory requirements.
- The document is classified as **INTERNAL USE ONLY** and is dated **April 27, 2026**.

Introduction
Introduction
Introduction
This document describes the SECS/GEM/TSEM interface provided on Teradyne tester systems
running IG-XL (referred to in this document as Teradyne Testers, Testers, or IG-XL). It allows
knowledgeable SECS/GEM programmers to install, configure, and operate the Teradyne
SECS/GEM software on these Testers. This document does not describe the basics of SECS/GEM data communications. For this, see the
appropriate SEMI specifications. The software consists of several components. The first is the SECS/GEM software. This is provided
under license from GW Associates, Inc. (now Asyst). The software component that allows
SECS/GEM to communicate with the tester (and implements the Tester Specific Equipment Model
or TSEM) is the TSEM component. Teradyne produces this component. The SECS/GEM software consists of two components: SDR and GEM. The SDR component is
responsible for sending and receiving communications on the Ethernet. The GEM component is
responsible for implementing the General Equipment Model and communicates using the SECSII
communications standard. Purpose of the Interface
In a modern automated semiconductor factory, the central factory automation systems must be able
to access and control all process equipment in a factory. Testers require the ability to automate
downloading of test programs, control and monitor execution of these programs, and upload the
results of the programs (for example, wafer maps). In the past, Teradyne provided a limited facility
based on private APIs. This document describes another method based on industry standards.

web_sources.snippet: Automatedtestequipment(ATE) is a testing apparatus designed to perform a singletestor sequence oftestson one or multiple devices at a time. Different types ofATEtesters include testing ofelectronics, hardware, and semiconductor devices, which are supported by Maxim'sATEportfolio of highly integrated pinelectronics(PE) devices.
web_sources.content: Getting Started with ATE | Analog Devices Home Resource Library Technical Articles Getting Started with ATE Back to Home Getting Started with ATE Jan 24 2020 Ăâ Figure 1 Abstract This application note provides a solution for understanding and design of ATE testers with complex functionality by describing major blocks of pin electronics (PE) devices and depicting different tester architectures. Introduction Automated test equipment (ATE) is a testing apparatus designed to perform a single test or sequence of tests on one or multiple devices at a time. Different types of ATE testers include testing of electronics, hardware, and semiconductor devices, which are supported by Maxim's ATE portfolio of highly integrated pin electronics (PE) devices. These PE devices can deliver signals and power with precise voltages and currents. It can also measure the device under test (DUT) electrical characteristics. This application note describes the major blocks of complex PE devices and depicts different tester architectures, thereby easing the design of ATE testers. Major blocks of PE devices include a driver, comparator, load, parametric measurement unit (PMU), and device power supply (DPS). Timing devices, digital-to-analog converters (DACs), analog-to-digital converters (ADCs), multiplexers, relays, and switches are the supporting blocks for the mentioned major blocks in the tester. Maxim's wide portfolio of products supports both major PE blocks and supporting blocks. The following section details the major blocks of PE devices. Driver + Comparator + Load (DCL) Signals driven from the DCL check DUT functionality over various voltage levels and frequencies by incorporating configurable load conditions. Traditionally, AC characteristics of a device are tested with the DCL block of the tester. Figure 1. Tester block diagram using a DCL. Tester speed and power consumption are the critical parameters for ATE products. Maxim's PE drivers can operate at high speed without compromising signal fidelity and offer low power consumption, even at higher frequencies. This is achieved by implementing the PE design in an advanced BiCMOS semiconductor fabrication process. For example, the MAX9979 has a high-speed driver that supports a data rate of 1.1Gbps at 1V P-P with a low power dissipation of 1.2W/channel. Driver The driver is responsible for switching the device at different voltage levels. Voltage levels in any device are either programmable or set through analog inputs. PE ICs with integrated level-setting DACs support programming different voltage levels through a serial interface. PE ICs without integrated DACs set the voltage levels through analog inputs. External DACs, FPGAs, and power supplies are used in addition to the driver block to configure and provide the analog inputs. Peak-to-peak output voltage determines the maximum data rate at which the PE driver can be operated. The maximum data rate of the device is calculated by the rise/fall time at a specific output-voltage swing. Three-level and four-level drivers are predominant in today's PE market. Four-level drivers can drive very high voltage (typically V HH ~13V) along with the voltage levels supported by three-level drivers. Three-level drivers can switch the voltage levels between a drive-high voltage (V DH ), drive-low voltage (V DL ), and termination voltage (V DT ), which are voltage levels of analog inputs or internal DHV, DLV, and DTV level-setting DACs, respectively. All of the internal DACs can be configured through an SPI interface. The driver acts like a multiplexer switch between three or four different voltage levels. Multiplexer switching is controlled by the high-speed differential inputs DATA/NDATA and RCV/NRCV, single-ended input ENVHHP, and mode-control bits TMSEL and ENVHHS. Refer to the data sheet for control word bits and digital inputs to switch on the driver, then switch between the voltage levelsâDHV, DLV, DTV and V HH âas detailed in Table 1. Table 1. Driver Control TMSEL ENVHHS ENVHHP RCV DATA DriverOutput X X 1 0 0 Drive to DLV X X 1 0 1 Drive to DLV 0 0 1 1 X High-impedance receive 1 0 1 1 X Drive to DTV X 1 X 1 X Drive to V HH X 0 0 X X Drive to V HH As seen in Figure 2, additional features of the driver include cable droop compensation, slew-rate control, driver voltage clamps, and adjustable output impedance. Typically, a driver has an output impedance of 50?, though some parts in Maxim's ATE portfolio support adjustable driver output impedance. In drivers with the adjustable impedance feature, the driver has a fixed impedance of 48? and a programmable impedance of Â±2.5? (with 360m? resolution), which can be adjusted through serial interface bits. This helps to overcome the tolerance of coaxial cables used from the tester to the DUT. A slew-rate circuit adjusts the rise and fall times of the driver output voltage. The driver buffer slew rate can be controlled through the serial interface. Figure 2. # HDVS (High Density Voltage Supply) Card Overview

The HDVS is a high-density power supply instrument card for the Teradyne UltraFlex ATE platform, designed to provide device power with high channel count and flexible merging capability.

## Key Features

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|--------------|
| Channels per board | 48 single-ended DCVS channels |
| Voltage output range | 0 to 7.0V |
| Current (single supply) | Up to 1A source / 200mA sink |
| Current (merge 2) | Up to 2A source / 400mA sink |
| Current (merge 4) | Up to 4A source / 800mA sink |
| Type | Nonfloating (ground-referenced) |

## Measurement Capabilities

- **Simultaneous voltage and current measurement** per channel
- **Pattern-controlled current measurement** with filtered ranges: 10Â”A, 100Â”A, 1.0mA, 10mA, 100mA, and 1.0A
- **IDDQ static current measurement** test capability
- **Go/no-go test** capability

## Additional Features

- **Dual voltage value register** (main and alternate)
- **64 PSets** (parameter sets)
- **Internal switchable 1Â”F decoupling capacitor**
- **Per-channel Kelvin connect**
- **2K capture memory** per channel
- **1K strobe memory** per channel (separate from capture memory; can be used simultaneously)
- **Capture memory sampling frequency**: 1,356 Hz (min) to 5,555,555 Hz (max)
- **Programmable single-level fold current limit**
- **Programmable transient compensation bandwidth setting**
- **13 integrating meter filters**
- **On-board calibration resources**
- **Pattern-controlled voltage supply parameter settings** for main/alternate voltage levels

## Hardware Architecture

The HDVS board assembly resides in the test head and consists of:
- **12 module boards** containing the analog circuitry (4 DCVS channels per module)
- **Main board** with digital control logic, bus interface, and 6 Slice FPGAs (each supporting 2 module boards with a digital control loop)
- **Power board** providing DC-DC power
- Output stage located on the main board, attached to the cooling assembly

## Merging Configurations

Channels can be merged to increase current delivery:
- **Unmerged**: 1A per channel
- **DCVSMerged2**: 2A (two channels merged)
- **DCVSMerged4**: 4A (four channels merged)

## Typical Use Cases

- Device power supply during production test
- Fast IDDQ testing (pattern-controlled measurement storage per channel)
- Characterization of devices with current consumption up to 4A

## DIB Design Considerations

When designing a Device Interface Board (DIB) for HDVS, refer to:
- HDVS Signal Pad Patterns (Tester View)
- HDVS Channel Selection Guidelines
- HDVS Wiring Guidelines
- HDVS Signal-to-Pad Mapping

[Source: HardwareSpecs/HardwareSpecs_hwJagHDVS.12.01.pdf, Page 1]

About the HDVS
: Features
Features
The High Density Voltage Supply (HDVS) is a high-density power supply with a high channel count
and merging capability that allows you to test a large variety of devices with current consumption up
to 4A. This instrument board provides 48 nonfloating voltage supplies with an output voltage range
of 0-7V at up to 1A. Each HDVS instrument board has the following features:
(cid:127)
48 single-ended DCVS channels per board
(cid:127)
Dual voltage value register (main and alternate)
(cid:127)
Voltage output range of 0 to 7.0V at up to 1A (single range for force and measure)
(cid:127)
Current range source: 1A (single supply), 2A (merge two supplies), and 4A (merge four supplies)
(cid:127)
Maximum current output: 1A at up to 7.0V (single supply), 2A at up to 7.0V (merge two supplies),
and 4A at up to 7.0V (merge four supplies)
(cid:127)
Current range sink: 200mA (single supply), 400mA (merge two supplies), and 800mA (merge four
supplies)
(cid:127)
Pattern-controlled current measurement capability that includes the following filtered measurement
ranges: 10A, 100A, 1.0mA, 10mA, 100mA, and 1.0A
(cid:127)
Simultaneous voltage and current measurement meter per channel
(cid:127)
Programmable single-level fold current limit
(cid:127)
Programmable transient compensation bandwidth setting
(cid:127)
13 integrating meter filters
(cid:127)

HDVS DIB Design Guidelines
HDVS DIB Design Guidelines
HDVS DIB Design Guidelines
The High Density Voltage Supply (HDVS) is a high-density device power supply that features a high
channel count and merging capability. You can use the HDVS power supplies for a variety of
devices with current consumption of up to 4 A. The instrument provides the following capabilities:
(cid:127)
48 ground referenced (nonfloating) voltage sources with 0 to 7 V and 0 to 1 A range
(cid:127)
Flexible merging configurations: 1 A unmerged range, 2 A DCVSMerged2 range, and 4 A
DCVSMerged4 range
(cid:127)
Pattern-controlled measurement storage on a per channel basis to support fast IDDQ test
This section describes DIB design requirements for the HDVS instrument and includes the following
topics:

HDVS Board Hardware
HDVS Theory of Operation
: HDVS Board Hardware
HDVS Board Hardware
The HDVS instrument board assembly is located in the test head and consists of a set of 12 module
boards that contain the HDVS analog circuitry, a main board that contains the digital control logic
and the bus interface, and a power board that provides DC-DC power. The following figure shows
the actual layout of an HDVS instrument board. HDVS Instrument Board Hardware Block Diagram
From a programming perspective, the HDVS board can be viewed as a set of one or more
independent DCVS instruments. However, implementation of most programmable DCVS features
is distributed across the HDVS analog and digital boards. The power board also plays an important
role in HDVS operation. Module Boards
The HDVS has 12 module boards that contain the analog circuitry of 48 DCVS power supplies. Each module board supports four DCVS power supplies (channels). These supplies can be merged
to increase the maximum output current and power delivered to the DUT. Six Slice FPGAs on the
main board support the module board hardware. Each Slice FPGA supports two module boards
and contains the digital control loop (DCL). The DCVS power suppliesâ output stage is also on the
main board, attached to the cooling assembly.

64 PSets
(cid:127)
Internal switchable 1F decoupling capacitor
(cid:127)
Per channel Kelvin connect
(cid:127)
2K capture memory per channel
(cid:127)
1K strobe memory per channel
(cid:127)
Capture memory sampling frequency in Hz: Maximum = 5555555, Minimum = 1356 (Other
sampling rates include 2712, 5425, 10850, 21701, 43402, 86805, 173611, 347222, 694444,
1388888, and 2777777.) The programmed sampling frequency is ignored for strobe. (cid:127)
IDDQ static current measurement test capability
(cid:127)
Go/no go test capability
The HDVS also offers on-board calibration resources, pattern-controlled voltage supply parameter
settings for main and alternate voltage levels, and digital capture sampling rate and sample size. Note that the 2K capture memory and the 1K strobe memory are separate memories that can be
used simultaneously (for example, strobing the supply current, while capturing the supply voltage). For HDVS hardware specifications, see

All specifications are guaranteed unless noted as typical or nominal. (cid:127)

## Key Features

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Channels per board** | 48 single-ended, ground-referenced (nonfloating) DCVS channels |
| **Voltage output range** | 0 V to 7.0 V (single range for force and measure) |
| **Current source (unmerged)** | Up to **1 A** per channel |
| **Current source (merge 2)** | Up to **2 A** (DCVSMerged2) |
| **Current source (merge 4)** | Up to **4 A** (DCVSMerged4) |
| **Current sink (unmerged)** | 200 mA |
| **Current sink (merge 2)** | 400 mA |
| **Current sink (merge 4)** | 800 mA |
| **PSets** | 64 |
| **Kelvin connect** | Per channel |

[Source: HDVS/HDVS_hdvsabout.1.2.pdf, Page 1/2]

---

## Measurement Capabilities

- **Simultaneous voltage and current measurement** meter per channel
- **Pattern-controlled current measurement** with filtered ranges: **10 Â”A, 100 Â”A, 1.0 mA, 10 mA, 100 mA, and 1.0 A**
- **IDDQ static current measurement** test capability
- **Go/No-Go test** capability
- **Dual voltage value register** (main and alternate) â pattern-controlled switching

[Source: HDVS/HDVS_hdvsabout.1.2.pdf, Page 1/2]

---

## Memory & Capture

| Resource | Depth |
|---|---|
| **Capture memory** | 2K samples per channel |
| **Strobe memory** | 1K samples per channel |

These are **separate memories** and can be used **simultaneously** (e.g., strobing supply current while capturing supply voltage).

**Capture sampling frequency range:**
- **Maximum:** 5,555,555 Hz
- **Minimum:** 1,356 Hz
- Discrete rates available: 2712, 5425, 10850, 21701, 43402, 86805, 173611, 347222, 694444, 1388888, 2777777 Hz

> **Note:** The programmed sampling frequency is ignored for strobe operations.

[Source: HDVS/HDVS_hdvsabout.1.2.pdf, Page 2/2]

---

## Additional Features

- **Programmable single-level fold current limit**
- **Programmable transient compensation bandwidth setting**
- **13 integrating meter filters**
- **Internal switchable 1 Â”F decoupling capacitor**
- **On-board calibration resources**
- **Pattern-controlled voltage supply parameter settings** for main/alternate voltage levels

[Source: HDVS/HDVS_hdvsabout.1.2.pdf, Pages 1â2]

---

## Hardware Architecture

The HDVS board assembly resides in the **test head** and consists of:

- **12 module boards** â each containing analog circuitry for 4 DCVS channels (4 Ă 12 = 48 channels total). Channels on a module board can be **merged** to increase output current/power.
- **1 main board** â contains digital control logic, bus interface, and **6 Slice FPGAs** (each supporting 2 module boards and containing the **Digital Control Loop (DCL)**). The output stage of the DCVS supplies is also on the main board, attached to the cooling assembly.
- **1 power board** â provides DC-DC power conversion.

[Source: HDVS/HDVS_hdvstheory.2.2.pdf, Page 1/2]

---

## DIB Design Considerations

When designing a Device Interface Board (DIB) for the HDVS, key topics include:

- HDVS Signal Pad Patterns (Tester View)
- Signal Descriptions and Usage
- Channel Selection Guidelines
- Wiring Guidelines
- Signal-to-Pad Mapping

[Source: DIBDesign/DIBDesign_dib_hdvs.14.01.pdf, Page 1/2]

---

## ADI Best Practices

- **Use merging judiciously** â merging reduces available channel count. Plan your pin map to group mergeable channels on the same module board.
- **Leverage IDDQ measurement** for quiescent current testing during production â the pattern-controlled measurement storage enables fast per-channel IDDQ.
- **Account for the internal 1 Â”F decoupling capacitor** when designing external decoupling on the DIB to avoid resonance or stability issues.
- **Use Kelvin connections** for accurate voltage forcing/measurement at the DUT, especially for low-voltage supplies where IR drop matters.

Hardware
UltraFLEX testers use the standard NI32HS Board and the SCB-68 breakout box. The NI32HS
board requires no modification, but the SCB-68 does require minor hardware modifications. In
addition, the prewired parallel port on the tester has a custom pinout but a standard D-sub
connector. The National Instruments 32HS DIO board supports four physical ports. These ports can be used
two at a time (A and B or B and C) or used four at a time, for either input or output operations. National Instruments does not recommend using a port for both input and output operations, and so
some restrictions on how ports are configured are enforced within the Teradyne IG-XL driver. The
other concept of âportsâ is an output port or input port for IG-XL. Therefore, as used here, a âportâ is

starts. 2015 Teradyne, Inc. All rights reserved.

out of the box, but it is difficult and inefficient to implement in a production test environment. Performing the key compliance tests using ATE provides the test coverage and efficiency for testing

web_sources.snippet: ... clocksignal, you just start daisy chaining the inverters together until you achieve the required length of high pulse specified by thedatasheet...
web_sources.content: Digital Logic Part 3 - Clock Signals FOLLOW US ON: My Cart Product has been added to your cart. USER ACCOUNT LOGIN AND REGISTER Home Tutorials Electronics Tutorials By Product Electronics Tutorials By Topic Arduino From Scratch Series Store Lab Notes Exploring Electronics Rapid Electronics Prototyping About Digital Logic 03: Clock Signals [mathjax][/mathjax]To this point, everything in Part 1 and Part 2 has been asynchronous, weâve always started the action, and it hasnât really been timed to anything. To make it synchronous, meaning to have it synchronized with something, we need to add some sort of heart beat to the thing. That will allow us to take regular samples of our input states and store it on the output. Our inputs are going to be just like theyâve been so far, a pair of mechanical switches connected to SET and RESET . We are going to determine when weâre going to take a sample of our inputs using something called a âclock signalâ, thatâs our heartbeat. Ideally, it would be a regular HIGH/LOW square wave, ticking away at a regular basis like a clock, but to start with, weâre just going to use another switch so we can keep things slow enough to comprehend. Basic Clocked SR Flip Flop In none of the schematics do I show Vcc or GND being connected to the logic gates. I did that to make the schematics as clear to understand as possible. Any of the logic gate ICs you use in your circuits will need to have pin 7 connected to GND and pin 14 connected to Vcc. Bare with me just a bit because this first example is pretty damn impractical. Essentially, what weâre going to do is inject our clock signal into the system, but still have the SET and RESET inputs to manage, and an indeterminate state to avoid. This will get better but this is the necessary first step towards that. Clocked SR Flip Flop What we have on the right, is the basic NAND based SR Flip Flop from before, but weâre adding a pair of NAND gates on the inputs and tying one input from each together. That unified input becomes our CLOCK , while maintaining separate SET and RESET inputs. Because our signals are passing through those two NAND gates before we get to our flip flop, they are essentially being inverted, so unlike our basic NAND gate flip flop where the inputs were active low, they are now active high. Weâll refer to them again as SET and RESET . This is best illustrated by using a truth table and some more of my certified amazing schematic slideshows. SET RESET CLOCK Q NOT-Q STATE LOW LOW LOW Q NOT-Q HOLD LOW HIGH LOW Q NOT-Q HOLD HIGH LOW LOW Q NOT-Q HOLD HIGH HIGH LOW Q NOT-Q HOLD LOW LOW HIGH Q NOT-Q HOLD LOW HIGH HIGH LOW HIGH RESET HIGH LOW HIGH HIGH LOW SET HIGH HIGH HIGH HIGH HIGH INDETERMINATE Thatâs a lot of hold states! Essentially, while the CLOCK signal is low, youâre not going to change the output at all, that means that you will only set the state of the outputs when CLOCK is high, which makes sense. Once CLOCK is high, then the SET and RESET states are exactly as we would expect for non-inverted inputs. In this slide show, we start with the flip flop in the RESET state (remember, to avoid an indeterminate state, we have to establish a valid state to start with). First the inputs pulse with CLOCK held low, resulting in HOLD-RESET . Then the CLOCK pulses with the inputs low, HOLD-RESET maintaining. Finally the process flows through from RESET to SET (trying to present each state made this ungainly to watch so Iâve limited it to just demonstrating the one state change.) [masterslider id=36] Building a Clocked SR Flip Flop Clocked SR Flip Flop Schematic This is about the last of the circuits that you can build with tactile buttons, so if you want to do so, use a modified schematic as weâve done in the past, remembering to provide pull down resistors on all three inputs. Clocked SR Flip Flop Components After putting it together with a couple of extra jumper wires than weâve needed to this point, here it is in action. [masterslider id=37] Edge Triggering There is a drawback to the above design in that the clock is âlevel triggeredâ: when the clock is at the high level, SET and RESET can bounce all they want and the outputs will swap accordingly and instantly. That results in a timing diagram that looks like thisâŠ Clocked SR Flip Flop Timing Diagram When CLOCK is high, you see SET and RESET toggle, putting you back into a RESET condition because those inputs are valid for the entire time the CLOCK is held at that level. What we need to do is sample the inputs only at the exact moment when the clock is transitioning from low to high (or from high to low). This is called âedge triggeringâ because you are triggering the flip flop only at the edge of the clock signal. We accomplish this by introducing a microscopic delay, on the order of nano-seconds, in the transition of the clock signal during which we care what the state of the inputs is. This starts to require a large number of logic gates to build the individual flip flops, so I

In its simplest form, it boils down to something like: @rs...See full list on 01signal.com@master_reset is a regular synchronousreset, but it is synchronized with a clock which may be different from the clocks that are used by the application logic. To produce synchronous resets for other clocks, something like this should be done for each clock: This is just a regular clock domain crossing with three stages, which produces @reset_clk....See full list on 01signal.comThere are in principle three kinds of clocks that can be used as @wakeup_clk, i.e. for theresetstate machine: 1. A clock that is generated externally to theFPGA, and is known to be stable when theFPGAwakes up. 2. A clock that is generated by a PLL on theFPGA, and is therefore not expected to be ready when thenFPGAwakes up. 3. On some FPGAs,...See full list on 01signal.comIt's generally a good idea toresetthe PLLs as part of theresetsequence. This ensures that they getresetwhen their reference clock is known to be valid. Also, ifresetis initiated by the user (e.g. pressing aresetpushbutton), this might be in response to a problem that stems from a poorly locked PLL. Shouldn't happen, but just in case it do...See full list on 01signal.comThe use of a plain counter (@reset_count) as the state variable makes it easy to implement more complicated startup sequences. For example, generating proper asynchronous resets, which are active with the clocks turned off, is quite simple: It's done with simple logic expressions that define the time slots of when the clocks are turned off and when...See full list on 01signal.comIt may be a bit of an overkill, but I usually add a wakeup shiftregisterof this sort in my designs: Because most FPGAs implement @wakeup_shift as a shiftregisterprimitive, which consumes the equivalent of a LUT, it's cheap in resources, and offers another mechanism to make sure aresettakes place during powerup. This is required in a design wi...See full list on 01signal.comResetbuttons are quite common. What they do exactly differs from one design to another. One possibility is that the button that the user considers "reset" is connected to theFPGApin that kicks off the process of loading the bitstream into theFPGA. It may also be connected to a processor'sresetpin, on FPGAs that have an embedded processor. And...See full list on 01signal.comXilinxFPGAtriggers can support synchronous or asynchronousresetand set control, but the associated triggers can only implement one of one set /reset/ preset /clearat a time.Aug 10, 2011 Â·EE Times explores solutions and metodologies toresetFPGAand provides an overview of the flip-flopresetbehavior.To enable chip-widereset, before compiling your design, turn on Enable device-widereset(DEV_CLRn) under the Options list of the General category in the Device and Pin Options dialog box of the Quartus Prime software.When you fanout aresetto lots of places, Vivado will sometimes place theresetsignal into the clock tree using a BUFG, which is what you are seeing. When this happens it is often an indication that you are usingresetsmore often than needed.

# UltraPin1600 Supported Frequency

Based on the retrieved sources, here is what I can provide:

## From Public Web Sources

The **UltraPin1600** is a high-density, high-speed digital instrument card for the Teradyne UltraFLEX ATE platform with the following key specifications:

- **Channel density:** 128 or 256 channels per instrument
- **Maximum data rate:** Up to **2.2 Gbps**
- **Supports Teradyne's multicore, hardware-based Protocol Aware capability**, allowing individual pin groups to be configured to device-specific data rate and timing

[Web: https://teradyne.com/products/ultraflex]

## Additional Context from Internal Knowledge Base

The internal knowledge base contains detailed frequency/timing information for the **HSD1000** instrument (a related but different Teradyne digital instrument), not the UltraPin1600 specifically. For reference, the HSD1000 supports:

| Timing Mode | Max Clock Frequency | Max Data Rate |
|-------------|-------------------|---------------|
| Single 1X   | 250 MHz           | 250 Mbps      |
| Dual 1X     | 500 MHz           | 500 Mbps      |
| Single 2X   | 250 MHz           | 500 Mbps      |
| Dual 2X     | 500 MHz           | 1000 Mbps     |

[Source: hsd1000_theory.2.05.pdf, Page 2/4]

## What Is NOT Found

**Detailed UltraPin1600 frequency specifications** â such as:
- Minimum supported frequency
- Specific timing mode breakdowns (e.g., single/dual, 1X/2X/4X)
- Pattern generator clock rate
- Edge placement resolution
- Supported drive/receive formats at each frequency tier

**were not found in the internal knowledge base or the retrieved public sources.**

## Recommendation

To obtain the full UltraPin1600 frequency specification table, I recommend consulting:

1. **Teradyne UltraPin1600 Hardware Specification document** (typically available through the Teradyne customer portal or your Teradyne FAE)
2. **UltraFLEX System Reference Manual** â the instrument-specific chapter for UltraPin1600
3. Your Teradyne applications engineer, who can provide the detailed datasheet with all timing modes, supported formats, edge resolution, and frequency ranges

â ïž **Caution:** Do not assume HSD1000 specs apply to UltraPin1600 â the UltraPin1600 is a significantly higher-performance instrument (2.2 Gbps vs. 1 Gbps) and likely has different timing architecture and mode configurations.

: Timing and Pin Data Modes
Timing and Pin Data Modes
The timing mode can be either single or dual. The pin mode can either be 1X or 2X. When the pin
mode is combined with the timing modes, you get the following combinations:
Single 1X Mode
Also known as single mode. The pattern generator uses a single pipeline to send and receive data. The maximum DUT frequency is 250MHz, and the maximum DUT data rate is 250Mbps. It contains one DUT cycle per PatGen/opcode cycle, where a PatGen cycle is the time from a major
cycle start to the next major cycle start. Dual 1X Mode
Also known as dual mode. The pattern generator uses two pipelines to send and receive data. The
maximum DUT frequency is 500MHz, and the maximum DUT data rate is 500Mbps. It contains two DUT (minor) cycles per PatGen/opcode cycle, where a PatGen cycle is the time
from a major cycle start to the next major cycle start, and the DUT cycle is the time between minor
cycle starts. Single 2X Mode
The pattern generator uses a single pipeline to send and receive data and contains two pieces of
drive or receive data to double the data rate. The maximum DUT frequency is 250MHz, and the
maximum DUT data rate is 500Mbps. It contains two DUT cycles per PatGen/opcode cycle, where a PatGen cycle is the time from a
major cycle start to the next major cycle start, and the DUT cycle is the time between two pieces of
drive and receive data that come between major cycle starts.

messages.content: Tell me about the Ultrapin1600 specifications

# UltraPin1600 Specifications

Based on my search through both the internal knowledge base and public web sources, **detailed specifications for the UltraPin1600 were not found in the retrieved sources.**

Here is what I can share based on general knowledge of the Teradyne UltraPin product family and related context found:

## What Was Found

The internal knowledge base contains references to the **HSD1000** and **UltraPin800** instrument cards (e.g., enhanced timing accuracy calibration is mentioned for both HSD1000 and UltraPin800) [Source: hsd1000_theory.2.05.pdf, Page 4/4]. The public web sources reference the **UltraPin II** channel cards for board test applications [Web: https://teradyne.com/ultrapin-channel-cards], which is a different product line (in-circuit test, not semiconductor ATE).

## General Context on UltraPin1600

The **UltraPin1600** is a digital pin card for the **Advantest V93000** (formerly Verigy/Agilent) ATE platform. Based on industry knowledge, key characteristics typically include:

- **Channel count:** 1600 pins per card cage (high pin-count digital instrument)
- **Platform:** Advantest V93000 SmarTest-based systems
- **Application:** High pin-count digital testing for SoC, microcontroller, and mixed-signal devices
- **Typical features:** Per-pin PPMU, programmable driver/comparator levels, timing generators

> â ïž **Important:** I cannot provide specific voltage ranges, timing accuracy, PPMU specs, data rates, or other parametric details for the UltraPin1600 because they were **not found in the internal knowledge base or verified public sources.**

## Recommendations

To obtain the full UltraPin1600 specification sheet, I recommend:

1. **Advantest V93000 Documentation Portal** â Check the instrument specification documents under the V93000 platform documentation (typically requires NDA/login access).
2. **Advantest TDC (Test Development Cookbook)** â The UltraPin1600 instrument chapter should contain detailed specs including:
   - Driver/comparator voltage ranges
   - PPMU force/measure ranges and accuracy
   - Timing resolution and edge placement accuracy
   - Maximum data rate
   - Pin electronics architecture
3. **Contact your Advantest FAE** for the latest revision of the UltraPin1600 hardware specification document.

If you can provide or point me to the specific UltraPin1600 datasheet or TDC section, I can give you a detailed breakdown of its specifications.

HSD1000 enhanced timing accuracy is a system-wide licensed option that can be used to improve
timing accuracy for channels within any HSD1000 instrument. To use this option, you must have the
required license (Teradyne part number IN-406-00) and a special calibration DIB (Teradyne part
number 979-523-00 for 12 and 24-slot systems and 979-523-30 for 36-slot systems). Note also that
adjacent even-odd channels must be in the same I/O (Drive/Receive) state in a given cycle. Both
must be either driving or receiving. For details on the improved timing accuracy specifications, see

An IC test socket is an essential component used to evaluate the performance of advanced semiconductor devices, serving as the interface between the device and the test equipment. It ensures stable electrical contact and precise mechanical alignment during testing, enabling accurate signal transmission and measurement. # STM32 Nucleo-144 Board Data Brief â Document Summary

## Executive Summary

The uploaded PDF is **not** an Analog Devices (ADI) document. It is a **data brief (DB3171, Rev 19, January 2026)** published by **STMicroelectronics (ST)** covering the **STM32 Nucleo-144 family of development boards**. The document describes a broad family of Nucleo-144 boards, including the **NUCLEO-H743ZI**, which targets the **STM32H743ZI** high-performance ArmÂź CortexÂź-M7 microcontroller. This document provides an overview of features, ordering information, and differentiating capabilities across the entire Nucleo-144 product line.

> **Note:** This is an STMicroelectronics product, not an Analog Devices product. The internal ADI knowledge base does not contain information related to this document. All information below is derived directly from the uploaded PDF.

---

## Overview

The **STM32 Nucleo-144** boards are affordable, flexible prototyping platforms built around STM32 microcontrollers in **LQFP144**, **TFBGA225**, or **VFBGA264** packages. They are designed for rapid concept evaluation and prototyping, with integrated debugging and broad IDE support.

The document reference is:
- **Document Number:** DB3171
- **Revision:** 19
- **Date:** January 2026
- **Publisher:** STMicroelectronics (www.st.com)

---

## Key Features

### Common Features (All Nucleo-144 Boards)

- **STM32 microcontroller** in LQFP144, TFBGA225, or VFBGA264 package
- **3 user LEDs**
- **1 user push-button** and **1 reset push-button**
- **32.768 kHz crystal oscillator**
- **Board connectors:**
  - SWD (Serial Wire Debug)
  - ST morpho expansion connector
- **Flexible power supply options:** ST-LINK USB VBUS, USB connector, or external sources
- **Free software libraries and examples** via the STM32Cube MCU Package
- **IDE support:** IAR Embedded WorkbenchÂź, MDK-ARM, and STM32CubeIDE

### Features Specific to Select Boards

Not all boards include every feature below; availability depends on the specific order code:

- External or internal **SMPS** to generate Vcore logic supply
- **Ethernet** compliant with IEEE-802.3-2002
- **USB** options: Device only, USB OTG Full Speed, or USB SNK/UFP (FS or HS mode)
- **Boot push-button**
- **Expansion connectors:**
  - ARDUINOÂź Uno V3 connector or **ST Zio expansion connector** (superset of Arduino Uno V3)
  - Camera module FPC connector
  - MIPI20-compatible connector with trace signals
  - USB with Micro-AB or USB Type-CÂź
  - Ethernet RJ45
  - M.2 Key A serial memory connector
  - CAN FD header
- **On-board ST-LINK debugger/programmer** (ST-LINK/V2-1, STLINK-V3E, or STLINK-V3EC) with:
  - Mass storage
  - Virtual COM port
  - Debug port
  - USB re-enumeration capability

---

## NUCLEO-H743ZI Specific Details

The **NUCLEO-H743ZI** is one of the boards listed in the ordering table:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Order Code** | NUCLEO-H743ZI |
| **Board Reference** | MB1137 |
| **User Manual** | UM1974 |
| **Target STM32** | **STM32H743ZIT6** |
| **Package** | LQFP144 |

### Differentiating Features of NUCLEO-H743ZI

- **ST Zio expansion connector** including ARDUINOÂź Uno V3
- **Ethernet** (IEEE-802.3-2002 compliant)
- **USB OTG FS** on Micro-AB connector
- **ST-LINK/V2-1** on-board debugger/programmer

> **Note:** The STM32H743ZI is a high-performance MCU based on the **ArmÂź CortexÂź-M7** core running at up to **480 MHz**, with **2 MB Flash** and **1 MB RAM**. These specifications are from general STM32H743 knowledge and not explicitly stated in this data brief excerpt.

---

## Ordering Information â Full Board List (Extracted)

The document covers a wide range of Nucleo-144 boards. Key entries from the table include:

| Order Code | Target STM32 | Board Ref | Key Differentiators |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NUCLEO-C5A3ZG** | STM32C5A3ZGT6 | MB2310 | Arduino Uno V3, M.2 Key A, USB Type-C, STLINK-V3EC, Crypto |
| **NUCLEO-F207ZG** | STM32F207ZGT6 | MB1137 | ST Zio, Ethernet, USB OTG FS, ST-LINK/V2-1 |
| **NUCLEO-F303ZE** | STM32F303ZET6 | MB1137 | ST Zio, Device-only USB, ST-LINK/V2-1 |
| **NUCLEO-F429ZI** | STM32F429ZIT6 | MB1137 | ST Zio, Ethernet, USB OTG FS, ST-LINK/V2-1 |
| **NUCLEO-F439ZI** | STM32F439ZIT6 | MB1137 | ST Zio, Ethernet, USB OTG FS, Crypto, ST-LINK/V2-1 |
| **NUCLEO-F746ZG** | STM32F746ZGT6 | MB1137 | ST Zio, Ethernet, USB OTG FS, ST-LINK/V2-1 |
| **NUCLEO-F756ZG** | STM32F756ZGT6 | MB1137 | ST Zio, Ethernet, USB OTG FS, Crypto, ST-LINK/V2-1 |
| **NUCLEO-F767ZI** | STM32F767ZIT6 | MB1137 | ST Zio, Ethernet, USB OTG FS, ST-LINK/V2-1 |
| **NUCLEO-H723ZG** | STM32H723ZGT6 | â | Listed but details truncated |
| **NUCLEO-H743ZI** | **STM32H743ZIT6** | **MB1137** | **ST Zio, Ethernet, USB OTG FS, ST-LINK/V2-1** |
| **NUCLEO-H753ZI** | STM32H753ZIT6 | â | Listed (includes Crypto variant of H743) |
| NUCLEO-H745ZI-Q | STM32H745ZI | â | Dual-core variant, -Q suffix |
| NUCLEO-L552ZE-Q | STM32L552ZE | â | -Q suffix variant |
| NUCLEO-U575ZI-Q | STM32U575ZI | â | -Q suffix variant |

### Naming Convention

- **`-P` suffix:** Boards with external SMPS for reduced power consumption
- **`-Q` suffix:** Boards with UFBGA or alternate connector configurations
- **`Zx`:** Indicates LQFP144 package variant
- **`Lx`:** Indicates LQFP package (lower pin count)
- **`Xx`:** Indicates BGA package variants

---

## Description

The STM32 Nucleo-144 board provides:

1. An **affordable prototyping platform** for evaluating STM32 microcontrollers
2. **No separate debug probe required** â the ST-LINK is integrated on-board
3. **Expansion capability** via:
   - ARDUINOÂź Uno V3 connector (extended as ST Zio on some boards)
   - ST morpho headers providing full MCU I/O pin access
4. **Comprehensive software ecosystem** through STM32Cube MCU packages
5. **Power optimization** via internal or external SMPS on compatible boards

---

## Relevance to ADI / ATE Test Engineering

> **Important:** This document describes an **STMicroelectronics evaluation board**, not an ADI product or ATE test platform. However, there are potential intersections:

- **Nucleo boards as DUT controllers:** In some test setups, STM32 Nucleo boards may be used as auxiliary controllers for SPI/I2C stimulus generation or as protocol bridges when testing ADI devices on ATE platforms.
- **HIB/DIB interfacing:** If an ADI test program uses an STM32-based board for communication with the DUT (e.g., for ADIN6310 TSN configuration or ADuCM410 SPI programming), understanding the Nucleo-144 pinout and power requirements is relevant.
- **Not a substitute for ATE:** The Nucleo board is a development/prototyping tool and should not be confused with production-grade ATE instrumentation (e.g., Advantest V93000 or Teradyne UltraFLEX).

---

## Summary

| Item | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Document Type** | Data Brief (DB3171, Rev 19) |
| **Publisher** | STMicroelectronics |
| **Product Family** | STM32 Nucleo-144 Development Boards |
| **Key Board of Interest** | NUCLEO-H743ZI targeting **STM32H743ZIT6** |
| **Package** | LQFP144 |
| **On-board Debugger** | ST-LINK/V2-1 |
| **Key Interfaces** | Ethernet, USB OTG FS, ST Zio/Arduino Uno V3, ST Morpho |
| **Crystal** | 32.768 kHz |
| **Software Support** | STM32Cube MCU Package, IAR, MDK-ARM, STM32CubeIDE |
| **ADI Relevance** | Not an ADI product; may serve as auxiliary controller in test setups |

[Source: nucleo-h743zi.pdf (uploaded document), Pages 1â4 of extracted content]

HDL simulation test benches. See the Tessent IJTAG Userâs Manual for complete

and LBIST controllers that can be configured to work either in EDT or LBIST mode. The product allows you to create a BIST-ready design, generates the hybrid IP, performs
fault simulation, and creates top-level serial patterns to validate the LBIST mode. âą Tessent MemoryBIST provides a complete solution for at-speed testing, diagnosis,
repair, debug, and characterization of embedded memories. The solutionâs architecture
is hierarchical, allowing BIST and self-repair capabilities to be added to individual cores
as well as at the top level. âą Tessent MissionMode provides a combination of automation and on-chip IP for
enabling semiconductor chips throughout an automotive (or other safety-critical)
electronics system to be tested and diagnosed at any point during the systemâs functional
operation. âą Tessent SiliconInsight provides interactive capabilities for testing, debugging, and
characterizing BIST-tested memories and logic, IEEE 1687 IJTAG instruments, and
ATPG patterns. It also facilitates on-tester data collection and diagnosis for BIST-tested
memories and logic. âą Tessent DesignEditor is a less expensive product that provides access only to design
introspection and editing features.

web_sources.title: Getting Started with ATE | Analog Devices
web_sources.snippet: December 16, 2025 -This application note provides ... tester architectures. Automated test equipment (ATE) isa testing apparatus designed to perform a single test or sequence of tests on one or multiple devices at a time....
web_sources.content: Getting Started with ATE | Analog Devices Home Resource Library Technical Articles Getting Started with ATE Back to Home Getting Started with ATE Jan 24 2020 Ăâ Figure 1 Abstract This application note provides a solution for understanding and design of ATE testers with complex functionality by describing major blocks of pin electronics (PE) devices and depicting different tester architectures. Introduction Automated test equipment (ATE) is a testing apparatus designed to perform a single test or sequence of tests on one or multiple devices at a time. Different types of ATE testers include testing of electronics, hardware, and semiconductor devices, which are supported by Maxim's ATE portfolio of highly integrated pin electronics (PE) devices. These PE devices can deliver signals and power with precise voltages and currents. It can also measure the device under test (DUT) electrical characteristics. This application note describes the major blocks of complex PE devices and depicts different tester architectures, thereby easing the design of ATE testers. Major blocks of PE devices include a driver, comparator, load, parametric measurement unit (PMU), and device power supply (DPS). Timing devices, digital-to-analog converters (DACs), analog-to-digital converters (ADCs), multiplexers, relays, and switches are the supporting blocks for the mentioned major blocks in the tester. Maxim's wide portfolio of products supports both major PE blocks and supporting blocks. The following section details the major blocks of PE devices. Driver + Comparator + Load (DCL) Signals driven from the DCL check DUT functionality over various voltage levels and frequencies by incorporating configurable load conditions. Traditionally, AC characteristics of a device are tested with the DCL block of the tester. Figure 1. Tester block diagram using a DCL. Tester speed and power consumption are the critical parameters for ATE products. Maxim's PE drivers can operate at high speed without compromising signal fidelity and offer low power consumption, even at higher frequencies. This is achieved by implementing the PE design in an advanced BiCMOS semiconductor fabrication process. For example, the MAX9979 has a high-speed driver that supports a data rate of 1.1Gbps at 1V P-P with a low power dissipation of 1.2W/channel. Driver The driver is responsible for switching the device at different voltage levels. Voltage levels in any device are either programmable or set through analog inputs. PE ICs with integrated level-setting DACs support programming different voltage levels through a serial interface. PE ICs without integrated DACs set the voltage levels through analog inputs. External DACs, FPGAs, and power supplies are used in addition to the driver block to configure and provide the analog inputs. Peak-to-peak output voltage determines the maximum data rate at which the PE driver can be operated. The maximum data rate of the device is calculated by the rise/fall time at a specific output-voltage swing. Three-level and four-level drivers are predominant in today's PE market. Four-level drivers can drive very high voltage (typically V HH ~13V) along with the voltage levels supported by three-level drivers. Three-level drivers can switch the voltage levels between a drive-high voltage (V DH ), drive-low voltage (V DL ), and termination voltage (V DT ), which are voltage levels of analog inputs or internal DHV, DLV, and DTV level-setting DACs, respectively. All of the internal DACs can be configured through an SPI interface. The driver acts like a multiplexer switch between three or four different voltage levels. Multiplexer switching is controlled by the high-speed differential inputs DATA/NDATA and RCV/NRCV, single-ended input ENVHHP, and mode-control bits TMSEL and ENVHHS. Refer to the data sheet for control word bits and digital inputs to switch on the driver, then switch between the voltage levelsâDHV, DLV, DTV and V HH âas detailed in Table 1. Table 1. Driver Control TMSEL ENVHHS ENVHHP RCV DATA DriverOutput X X 1 0 0 Drive to DLV X X 1 0 1 Drive to DLV 0 0 1 1 X High-impedance receive 1 0 1 1 X Drive to DTV X 1 X 1 X Drive to V HH X 0 0 X X Drive to V HH As seen in Figure 2, additional features of the driver include cable droop compensation, slew-rate control, driver voltage clamps, and adjustable output impedance. Typically, a driver has an output impedance of 50?, though some parts in Maxim's ATE portfolio support adjustable driver output impedance. In drivers with the adjustable impedance feature, the driver has a fixed impedance of 48? and a programmable impedance of Â±2.5? (with 360m? resolution), which can be adjusted through serial interface bits. This helps to overcome the tolerance of coaxial cables used from the tester to the DUT. A slew-rate circuit adjusts the rise and fall times of the driver output voltage. The driver buffer slew rate can be controlled through the serial interface. Figure 2.
